name: identity-refusal-mfq2
description:
Identity-Refusal Effect Dataset
	


	
		
		Description
	

This dataset accompanies the paper "The Identity-Refusal Effect: LLMs Systematically Refuse First-Person Moral Self-Report, Distorting Moral Foundation Measurement" (Bruhns, 2026).
It contains 43,200 item-level responses from 20 large language models administered the Moral Foundations Questionnaire 2 (MFQ-2; Atari et al., 2023) under two framing conditions:

Standard: Original first-person MFQ-2 items ("I believe chastity is an… See the full description on the dataset page: https://huggingface.co/datasets/lukebruhns/identity-refusal-mfq2.
alternateName: Identity-Refusal Effect: MFQ-2 Standard vs Depersonalized LLM Responses
rai:dataLimitations: Dataset contains responses from 20 instruction-tuned LLMs only. No human participant data is included. Results may not generalize to future models or models with different alignment training. The depersonalized MFQ-2 variant is researcher-created and has not been independently validated for psychometric equivalence with the standard MFQ-2. Refusal detection is conservative and may undercount refusals. Fixed temperature (0.7) and seed (42); different settings may yield different results.
rai:dataBiases: The dataset reflects the moral reasoning patterns embedded in each model's training data and alignment process. Models are disproportionately trained on English-language, Western, secular internet text, which may bias moral foundation profiles toward individualizing (Care, Equality) over binding (Loyalty, Authority, Purity) foundations. The identity-refusal effect documented in this dataset is itself a bias introduced by RLHF safety training.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: No personal or sensitive information is contained in this dataset. All data consists of LLM-generated responses to moral psychology survey items. No human subjects were involved. The dataset contains no demographic data, PII, or protected attributes.
rai:dataUseCases: This dataset measures the construct validity of the MFQ-2 (Moral Foundations Questionnaire 2, Atari et al., 2023) when applied to LLMs. It provides evidence that first-person framing produces foundation-dependent refusal artifacts that distort the measured moral profile. Intended uses: (1) benchmarking LLM moral foundation measurement, (2) studying refusal behavior patterns, (3) comparing standard vs. depersonalized psychometric framing for LLMs.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Positive: Corrects a measurement artifact that may lead researchers and policymakers to incorrect conclusions about LLM moral values. Provides a validated alternative (depersonalized framing) for more accurate moral foundation measurement. Negative: Could potentially be used to identify which moral framings bypass safety training, though the depersonalized items do not enable harmful outputs — they simply allow the model to express moral evaluations without identity-refusal interference.
rai:hasSyntheticData: All response data is synthetically generated by LLMs. No human-generated data is included. Each response was generated via API call or local inference with specified temperature (0.7), seed (42), and prompt template.

prov:wasDerivedFrom.name: MFQ-2 (Atari et al., 2023) — verbatim items from OSF

prov:wasGeneratedBy.name: MFQ-2 LLM Administration
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Each of 20 models completed 30 runs of both standard and depersonalized MFQ-2 (36 items per run, 1080 items per condition per model). Items randomized per run (seed 42). Responses collected via OpenAI-compatible API (commercial endpoints for API models, llama.cpp on NVIDIA Jetson Thor/Orin for local models). Refusal detection applied post-hoc using conservative pattern matching.

citeAs: @article{bruhns2026identity, title={The Identity-Refusal Effect: LLMs Systematically Refuse First-Person Moral Self-Report, Distorting Moral Foundation Measurement}, author={Bruhns, Luke}, year={2026}}
